skill.name: web-performance-seo
skill.description: Fix PageSpeed Insights and Lighthouse accessibility failures caused by contrast audit issues in modern CSS.
skill.summary: Diagnose and fix Lighthouse contrast audit failures that can block accessibility and SEO scoring.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences as shell execution and flagged weak cryptography from non-cryptographic text. Review found no executable scripts, network access, credential handling, prompt injection, or destructive behavior. The only remaining risk is that users may run read-only local search commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Treated as Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are Markdown code fences containing read-only ripgrep examples and CSS or TSX remediation examples. They are not executable skill code, and no command injection path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains documentation only, and the command examples are limited to local text search with rg. No script, variable interpolation, network transfer, or file modification is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts occur in descriptive text and remediation guidance, not in cryptographic code. No encryption, hashing, certificate handling, or security protocol logic appears in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines discuss PageSpeed SEO and visual filters. I did not find evidence of DES, MD5, SHA1, or any implemented cryptographic operation.

content.value_statement: Lighthouse and PageSpeed Insights can show unclear accessibility failures when contrast audits cannot complete. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through targeted CSS checks and safe remediation patterns.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common CSS patterns that break Lighthouse color contrast audits.
Suggests safer replacements for CSS filters, blend modes, and low-opacity backgrounds.
Guides conversion from OKLCH or OKLAB colors to HSL or RGB values.
Provides fallback patterns for gradient text and forced-colors users.
Defines acceptance criteria for contrast ratios and numeric accessibility scores.
limitations: Does not run Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights, or axe by itself.
Does not calculate exact contrast ratios for every color pair.
Does not replace manual review for brand color and design decisions.
Does not guarantee immediate PageSpeed Insights updates after deployment.

use_cases.title: Repair a Broken Lighthouse Accessibility Score
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to find CSS patterns that cause a non-numeric accessibility result.

use_cases.title: Prepare an SEO Release Check
use_cases.description: Review contrast risks before deployment so accessibility signals do not block SEO validation.

use_cases.title: Improve Design System Contrast Rules
use_cases.description: Turn recurring fixes into stable color, opacity, and text-overlay rules.

prompt_templates.title: Find Likely Contrast Audit Triggers
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this frontend project for CSS patterns that can break Lighthouse color contrast audits. Focus on filters, OKLCH or OKLAB colors, low opacity, gradient text, and text over images.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a PageSpeed Insights Exclamation Mark
prompt_templates.prompt: PageSpeed Insights shows an exclamation mark for accessibility instead of a score. Help me identify likely contrast audit failures and list the files I should inspect first.

prompt_templates.title: Plan CSS Contrast Remediation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized remediation plan for the contrast issues in this app. Include low-risk replacements for filters, transparent text, weak overlays, and modern color syntax.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Accessibility SEO Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the proposed CSS changes against Lighthouse and WCAG contrast expectations. Identify remaining risks before I deploy and rerun PageSpeed Insights.

output_examples.input: Lighthouse shows an accessibility exclamation mark after adding a blurred card overlay.
output_examples.output: The report would flag backdrop blur and low opacity as likely triggers. It would recommend a more opaque background and a local Lighthouse retest.

output_examples.input: A hero heading uses gradient text with transparent color.
output_examples.output: The report would recommend a solid fallback color, a limited gradient mode, and a forced-colors override.

output_examples.input: A design token uses OKLCH colors across the theme.
output_examples.output: The report would identify OKLCH as a compatibility risk for some audits and suggest equivalent HSL or RGB tokens.

best_practices: Run local Lighthouse or axe checks before waiting for PageSpeed Insights updates.
Use solid fallback colors for decorative gradient or image-backed text.
Keep overlays opaque enough to meet contrast requirements across responsive breakpoints.
anti_patterns: Treating an accessibility exclamation mark as a low score instead of an audit failure.
Using transparent gradient text without a readable fallback color.
Relying on blurred or low-opacity backgrounds behind important text.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps diagnose and fix Lighthouse or PageSpeed Insights contrast audit failures that can hide the accessibility score.

faq.question: Does it run PageSpeed Insights automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a workflow and remediation patterns. You still run Lighthouse, axe, or PageSpeed Insights separately.

faq.question: Why are OKLCH and OKLAB mentioned?
faq.answer: Some audit environments may handle modern color syntax poorly. Converting key tokens can make contrast checks more reliable.

faq.question: Can it improve SEO directly?
faq.answer: It supports SEO by helping accessibility audits complete successfully. It does not replace broader SEO analysis.

faq.question: Is this only for Tailwind projects?
faq.answer: No. Some examples use Tailwind-style opacity, but the guidance applies to CSS, TSX, and component-based frontends.

faq.question: What should I verify after changes?
faq.answer: Verify that accessibility shows a numeric score and that normal text reaches 4.5:1 contrast where required.
